content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Most e-commerce search bars are built for people who already know exactly what they want. Type “men’s slim-fit cotton crewneck t-shirt, sage green, size M” — and you’ll get results.
Type “something green and casual for summer” — and you’ll get a mess of unrelated products, mis-sorted filters, and a bounce rate that keeps the analytics team up at night.
That gap between how people shop in real life and how they’re forced to shop online is the problem SapientPro was brought in to solve.
The Client’s Setup
The client runs a mid-to-large e-commerce operation across the Netherlands and Switzerland, built on ShopWare. Their product catalog runs deep. Their customer base is broad.
And like most online retailers, they had a search experience that worked fine for power users and frustrated everyone else.
SapientPro had already worked with this client for several years on platform development. The relationship was built on trust and a shared technical vocabulary.
So when the team identified an opportunity to test an AI-powered shopping assistant, the client was willing to run the experiment.
The brief was simple in concept: build something that works the way a good salesperson works. You describe what you’re looking for, loosely or precisely, and it finds what you need.
Five Problems That Didn’t Have Easy Answers
Before a single line of code was written, the team had to account for five distinct failure points.
The first was language.
Customers rarely describe products the way product catalogs describe products. “Something cozy for a camping trip” and “fleece mid-layer jacket” mean the same thing, but a keyword search treats them as entirely different queries.
The second was choice overload.
When every filter is manual and every result page looks the same, customers stop trying. The average online shopper gives up faster than most retailers realize — and a wider catalog only makes it worse.
The third was the absence of any conversational layer.
A knowledgeable floor associate at a clothing store asks follow-up questions. “Are you looking for something to wear inside or outside?” “What’s your budget?” “Do you prefer a zip or a pullover?” No e-commerce search engine does that. There was no mechanism in place to replicate that back-and-forth.
The fourth was a technical constraint: vector search engines, which handle semantic queries well, tend to fall apart when you need real filtering logic on top of them.
Early experiments with Qdrant confirmed this. Semantic flexibility and structured filtering turned out to be harder to combine than expected.
The fifth was integration.
Whatever got built had to slot into the existing ShopWare infrastructure without disrupting what was already working. The client’s team builds features independently too, so the module had to be clean enough for them to extend on their own.
What Got Built
The assistant runs on a hybrid search architecture. At its core, an LLM interprets the user’s message and decides how to handle it: does this call for keyword matching, semantic search, or both? That decision happens automatically, below the surface, in under a second.
On the semantic side, the team switched from Qdrant to PostgreSQL with pgvector. The move gave them finer control over filtering and aggregation while keeping the fuzzy-matching capability intact. A query like “a green T-shirt” now surfaces light green, emerald, and turquoise options based on what’s actually in stock — not just items where the word “green” appears in the product title.
On the structured side, the assistant handles filters natively. Ask it “How many green T-shirts are available?” and it returns “5 options found.” Ask for products under a certain price, or in a specific size, and it applies those constraints directly. It also handles pagination without the user having to think about it.
When a query is too vague to return useful results, the assistant asks. Not in a frustrating, form-filling way — in the way a person would. “Are you looking for something more casual or formal?” That one question often eliminates 80% of irrelevant results before they’re ever displayed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ion Grytsku, Head of Backend Development at SapientPro, described the design intent this way: “It feels like chatting with a knowledgeable store associate, not a scripted bot. Tight prompt design holds relevance, handles fuzzy queries, and guides shoppers to smarter buys.”
The whole thing was delivered in 180 hours, with a one-person team.
Why the Architecture Decision Mattered
The Qdrant-to-PostgreSQL switch is worth pausing on, because it illustrates a broader pattern in AI development work.
Qdrant is a purpose-built vector database. It handles semantic similarity well. But when you need to combine that with real-time inventory data, price filtering, color aggregation, and availability checks — all in a single query, in under a second — a specialized vector database creates more complexity than it solves.
PostgreSQL with pgvector gives you semantic search as an extension on top of a database that’s already good at structured queries. The tradeoff is that you’re asking one system to do more. The benefit is that the two modes of search — keyword and semantic — share the same data, the same filtering logic, and the same response layer. No synchronization lag. No dual-system debugging.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

For a real-time shopping assistant where a one-second response time is the target (and the delivered result), that architecture decision was the difference between a working product and a finicky prototype.
The Outcome
The assistant went live as a proof of concept on the client’s ShopWare platform. Query response time came in at under one second. The conversational layer handled vague inputs, applied filters on structured queries, and prompted follow-up questions when the request needed more context.
More practically: a customer could open the chat, type “something for a camping weekend, not too expensive,” and get a useful, filtered set of results. No manual filter-setting. No reformatting of their natural request into catalog language. No wading through 47 identical-looking jacket listings.
That’s what the in-store experience actually is — and it had been missing from online retail for a long time.

SapientPro is a custom software development company specializing in AI development, SaaS, Web3, and e-commerce. If you’re building something in this space, you can reach the team at sapient.pro.
